title: Happy City: Transforming Our Lives Through Urban Design
excerpt: A fascinating exploration of the intersection between urban design and human happiness, blending psychology, neuroscience, and urban studies to ask how cities can make us happier.
keyIdeas: Urban design directly affects psychological well-being through social connection, stress, autonomy, and daily experience
Car-centric suburban sprawl isolates people and reduces happiness despite promising the 'good life'
Dense, walkable, mixed-use neighborhoods with abundant public space increase social connection and life satisfaction
The design of streets, parks, transit, and housing shapes our social networks, stress levels, and sense of belonging
keyTakeaways: The suburban dream promised freedom but delivered isolation, long commutes, and financial stress
Social connection is the single strongest predictor of happiness, and urban design can either foster or inhibit it
Commuting is uniquely destructive to happiness — longer commutes consistently reduce life satisfaction
Cities that prioritize people over cars — through walking, cycling, and public transit — produce happier citizens
whoShouldRead: Urban planners and city officials looking for evidence-based design principles
Anyone interested in the science of happiness and how environment shapes well-being
Suburban dwellers questioning whether the suburban lifestyle is actually making them happy
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a technical planning manual — this is a popular science book, not a design guide
Those who strongly prefer suburban living and are not interested in alternative perspectives
Anyone looking for an economics-focused analysis of cities — the lens here is psychological well-being
